e. the City Accountant and the Supply Officer to:
e.1 reconcile the Report of the Physical Count of Inventories with the GL
controlling accounts; and
e.2 draw a Journal Entry Voucher to record the adjustments.

We reiterated our recommendations that the City Mayor instruct the:

a. Inventory Committee to conduct the physical count of all PPE accounts to
determine the existence and completeness of the PPE owned by the City,
prepare the RPCPPE and submit the same to the Audit Team for further
verification.
b. City Accountant and the CGSO to:
b.1 reconcile the amount of PPE per Accounting records with the RPCPPE to
ascertain the accuracy of the PPE account balances appearing in the
financial statements;
b.2 verify , analyze and evaluate the causes of discrepancies, if any; and
b.3 make the necessary adjustments/corrections in the book of accounts and
property records for the discrepancies noted.
c. CGSO to include in the RPCPPE the condition of all property of the City and if
found unserviceable, prepare the Inventory and Inspection Report of
Unserviceable Property (IIRUP) and other necessary documents for proper
disposal of these property.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the:

a. City General Services Officer to:
a.1 maintain a complete Local Road Inventory and Road Map;
a.2 keep a Local Road Network Property Card (LRNPC) for all roads and their
components; and
a.3 render a complete Report on Local Road Networks at the end of the year.
b. Inventory Committee, in coordination with the City Engineering Office, to
conduct physical inventory of all existing local road networks in the City,
prepare the corresponding Report on the Physical Count of Local Road
Network to be reconciled in the accounting records and LRNPC, and furnish a
copy of the inventory report to the Audit Team for verification and audit;
c. City Accountant to:
c.1 identify the road lots recorded under the Land account and effect their
reclassification to the Road Networks account;
c.2 reconcile the list of inventory per local road inventory report and road map
with those recorded in the Registry in Public Infrastructure;
c.3 keep and maintain subsidiary records for road and road components for
every road network;
c.4 segregate and recognize the cost of each component of the local roads
with identified cost;
c.5 prepare a lapsing schedule for the computation of the depreciation for each
component at the end of the year; and
c.6 disclose the total road network system in the Notes to Financial Statements
in the format prescribed under COA Circular No. 2015-008.
d. City Engineer to provide the City Accountant and the City General Services
Officer with the complete description and cost segregation of road
components for road projects.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the:

a. City Engineer to:
a.1 submit to COA the Quarterly Reports on Government PPAs with complete
details such as target completion date, percentage of completion, total cost
incurred to date and project status, copy furnish the City Accountant as basis
for recording / adjustment in the books and/or recognize / classify the
completed projects to its appropriate account;
b. City Accountant to:
b.1 reconcile the ledger balance of the CIP accounts with the Quarterly Report
on Government PPAs submitted by the CEIDO and record the necessary
adjustments, if any; and
b.2 draw a Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to recognize to its appropriate asset
account/s the completed project totaling P1,497,734.18 and record the
corresponding provision for depreciation; and adjust/correct the account
Construction in Progress – Infrastructure Assets.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct:

a. the City Accountant, in coordination with the Acting City Treasurer, to
reconcile the outstanding balance of Loans Payable – Domestic in the financial
statements with the balance in the Annual Statement of Indebtedness,
Payments and Balances prepared by the City Treasury Management Office,
and prepare all necessary adjusting/correcting entries; and
b. the City Accountant to classify the outstanding balance of the long-term loans
payable in the Statement of Financial Position by re-computing the current and
non-current portions of the loan payable based on the Amortization Schedule
provided by the Bank and prepare the necessary adjusting/correcting entries.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to:

a. exert effort to retrieve the documents to support the dormant accounts;
b. prepare the necessary correcting/adjusting entries on all misclassified account
balances and errors in recording of prior years’ income; and
c. coordinate with the City Treasurer and City Engineer for possible confirmation
from various contractors with outstanding retention money and/or the possible
reversion to unappropriated surplus of the 10 percent retention money totaling
to P4,355,925.86, which remained outstanding for two to five years.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Accountant to:

a. determine the amount of fund transfers from the GF Proper to Special
Accounts in the GF particularly in regard to CCC to ascertain the adjusted
Cash balance as of December 31, 2020 and draw Journal Entry Vouchers
(JEVs) for the necessary correcting entries; and
b. see to it that fund transfer from the General Fund Proper to Special Accounts
in the GF, and vice versa is recorded everytime there is a transaction involve
therein;
c. prepare financial statements for each special account in the GF so that
management may be adequately informed on the result of its operation for
appropriate action and submit the same to the Audit Team; and
d. maintain special accounts for Cemetery operation, in compliance with the
aforementioned regulations.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant and the City
Treasurer to submit immediately to the Office of the Auditor the 42 DVs and their
supporting documents to ascertain the validity, legality and propriety of
transactions and henceforth, require submission of accounts within the first 10
days of the ensuing month as required by COA Circular 2009-006.

We recommended that the City Mayor, as the Chairman of the Local School Board:

a. require the City Treasurer and City Accountant to submit the actual and
estimated income cited under the provisions of 5.5 of DEPED-DBM-DILG JC
No. 1, s. 2017;
b. ensure that the appropriations/budgets for the proposed PAPs included in the
Annual and Supplemental Investment Plan are aligned with the net amount
available for appropriation as shown in SEF Budget Preparation Form No. 1
(Annex A) of 5.2.2 of the DEPED-DBM-DILG JC No. 1, s. 2017;
c. present the SEF budget by program, activity, project, allotment class and per
school as required under the provisions of 5.2.2 of the DEPED-DBM-DILG JC
No. 1, s. 2017;
d. see to it that the proposed PAPs included in the AIP and SIP are compliant
with the policies on the use of SEF to support the needs of the schools and
alternative learning modalities due to COVID-19 thereby, efficient and
maximum utilization of SEF can be achieved.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the Acting City Treasurer and Market
Supervisor to:

a. initiate legal remedies on all stallholders occupying the stalls without Contract
of Lease;
b. maintain an updated subsidiary ledger cards for every individual stallholders
indicating therein surcharges and penalties assessed or computed for the non-
payment of monthly stall rentals;
c. be more aggressive and persistent in collecting the monthly stall rentals,
penalties and surcharges; and
d. strictly enforce the above cited provisions of the Market Code of the City of
Calamba.

We recommended that the City Mayor:

a. direct the Acting City Treasurer, in coordination with the Market Supervisor, to
reconcile the noted discrepancy of P113,212.38 for the total stall rentals
collected in CY 2020 and notify the City Accountant of the reconciled amounts
for the preparation of necessary adjustments in the books of accounts; and
b. require the Acting City Treasurer and Market Administration to maintain
individual stallholder’s ledger cards to ascertain the correctness of the reports
submitted by the Market Supervisor, as well as, the records of the City
Treasury Office and City Accounting Office.

We recommended that the City Mayor:

a. require the City Accountant and City General Services Officer (CGSO) to see to
it that all procurements and disbursements of the City are supported with
complete documentary requirements to establish the validity of the
transactions;
b. instruct the CGSO to submit to the Auditor all the lacking documents
pertaining to the above-cited transactions to determine the propriety of these
transactions; and
c. see to it that subsequent procurement of monoblock chairs must bear
markings like “City Government of Calamba” for proper identification of
accountability.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Planning and Development
Coordinator to:

a. conduct a thorough study/investigation and survey of areas/location of the
proposed projects, in coordination with the barangays and other concerned
officials, prior to inclusion of the PPAs in the City’s AIP under the 20 percent
LGDF in order to guarantee the full implementation of the PPAs in the
projected/scheduled year/date to be completed;
b. see to it that the PPAs included in the AIP have complete descriptions by
indicating, among others, the specific location, area, purpose, and period of
implementation;
c. coordinate with the Local Development Council to evaluate and reassess the
PPAs under the continuing appropriations if these PPAs are still responsive to
existing needs of its constituents, otherwise, initiate reprogramming of PPAs
to more viable and significant PPAs to meet the socio-economic needs of the
City; and
d. request the City Engineer to fast track the on-going development projects and
closely monitor the implementation of the same so that any problems in the
course of implementation can be immediately addressed.

We recommended that the City Mayor:

a. require the City Accountant, in coordination with the City Environment and
Natural Resources Officer (CENRO), to submit the aforecited documents to the
Auditor for audit purposes;
b. instruct S.B Hain Enterprises and General Services, Inc. that all Requests for
Payment must be supported with Delivery Receipts/Invoices indicating the
volume of solid waste collections and plate number of vehicle used, as
stipulated in the contract; and
c. require the CENRO to designate a person responsible for checking the
accuracy of the data indicated in the Delivery Receipts/Invoices.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct:

a. the City Accountant and the OIC – City Disaster Risk Reduction and
Management Officer to reconcile the total unutilized balance of DRRMF at year-
end, revise the Report on Utilization of DRRMF and prepare adjusting entries, if
necessary; and
b. the City Accountant, in coordination with the City Treasurer, to transfer
immediately to the Special Trust Fund account the total unutilized balance of
the DRRMF as of December 31, 2020 and every year thereafter, in compliance
with COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012.

We recommended that the City Mayor:

a. direct the City Disaster Risk Reduction Management Council to identify the
needs of the City in time of calamities and disasters as guide in the
preparation of an accurate LDRRMFIP and ensure the maximum utilization of
the LDRRMF; and
b. require the CDRRM Officer to closely monitor the implementation of the
proposed PPAs and institutionalize measures to strengthen disaster
preparedness and response capabilities at all levels to answer the needs of its
constituents in time of calamities and disasters.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the GAD Focal Point Person, GADO,
City Budget Officer and City Accountant:

a. submit to the Office of the Auditor a written justifications or gender issues
relative to the expenses totaling P1,443,129.72 charged against the City’s GAD
fund; and
b. implement strictly the provisions/guidelines set under PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA
JMC No. 2013-01 and its amendments.

We recommended that the Management submit written justifications on the noted
deficiencies for the implementation of the Social Amelioration Program of the
DSWD and submit all necessary documents as required by Memorandum Circulars
of the DSWD to support the receipt of the subsidy and the qualification of the
beneficiaries.

Moreover, instruct the concerned office/s to initiate the return / refund of the
overpayment made to 339 beneficiaries amounting to P2,203,500.00.

We recommended that the City Mayor:

a. direct the CSWDO to post the complete lists of Cash Assistance Program (CAP)
beneficiaries in the official website of the City;
b. instruct the CSWDO and CTO to submit written explanations/justifications on
the noted deficiencies; and
c. direct the City Budget Officer and Acting City Treasurer to reconcile their
records, and inform the City Accountant to effect the necessary adjusting
entries in the books of accounts, if warranted.

We have recommended that management require Southwestern Pension House and ML Descallar Corporation to submit the request for payment including the detailed statement of account showing the names of quarantined individuals and date/number of days quarantined and daily rental rate; and
Clearly indicate the rental rates on the MOA for future similar transactions.

We have recommended that management instruct the MDRRMO thru coordination with concerned municipal officials to submit to the OCD and the COA a complete list of donations received and distributed by the municipality in accordance with Section II-A.2 of COA Circular No. 2020-009.

We have recommended that management:
Organize a validation team who will work in close coordination with barangay officials concerned and the DSWD to conduct verification on the eligibility of the aforementioned beneficiaries based on the filled-up SAC;
Direct the validation team to submit to the audit team a report/status of their verification and the actions taken by management; and
Confer with DSWD – Regional office No. 10 on the results of the revalidation and obtain a written advise as to the course of action if recipients were declared to be ineligible.

We have recommended that management direct all concerned officials to provide the necessary and relevant specifications of the items to be procured in clear and unambiguous terms for all future procurement activities in order to determine the specific requirements of the end users and clearly define the deliverables of the supplier.

We have recommended that management  require the BAC to observe strict compliance on the mandatory posting of the procurement data for all contracts awarded under Emergency Procurement under the Bayanihan Act pursuant to GPPB Circular No. 01-2020.

We have recommended that management effect the necessary increase of the fidelity bond of the Accountable Officers commensurate to their actual accountabilities, in accordance with Treasury Circular No. 02-2009, in order to provide adequate protection on the Municipality’s resources.

We have recommended that management effect the payment of goods and services through check payable to the supplier or dealer except in cases where needed items are extremely necessary and urgent which cannot be conveniently paid by check and stop paying thru cash advance for predetermined expenditures where direct payment to suppliers is possible.

We reiterated our prior year’s audit recommendation that management instruct the Municipal Assessor to secure and commence the processing of the TCT of all the land properties, and seek budgetary allocations for the pertinent cost thereof; and
Direct the Municipal Assessor to provide copies of TCT to the Municipal Accountant, as basis for the latter to establish a subsidiary list and as basis to elect the revaluation model in accounting for Land as prescribed under IPSAS 17 using latest available Fair Market Value.

We reiterated our prior year’s audit recommendations that management:
Conduct actual physical inventory-taking of the properties and prepare Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE);
Require the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to maintain Property Cards, Property, Plant and Equipment Ledger Cards for each category of assets, and Real Property Ledger Cards for land; and
Require them further to conduct periodic reconciliation of the ledger card balances in order to achieve accuracy and fair presentation of the financial statements.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to draw the adjusting journal entry at year-end to record all staled checks, to wit:

Accounts Payable Pxxx

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendations that the City Mayor instruct the: a. Inventory Committee to conduct the physical count of all PPE accounts to determine the existence and completeness of the PPE owned by the City, prepare the RPCPPE and submit the same to the Audit Team for further verification. b. City Accountant and the CGSO to: b.1 reconcile the amount of PPE per Accounting records with the RPCPPE to ascertain the accuracy of the PPE account balances appearing in the financial statements; b.2 verify and evaluate the causes of discrepancies, if any; and b.3 make the necessary adjustments/corrections in the book of accounts and property records for the discrepancies noted. c. CGSO to include in the RPCPPE the condition of all property of the City and if found unserviceable, prepare the Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Property (IIRUP) and other necessary documents for proper disposal of these property.

Accordingly, the RPCPPE was updated, thereby discrepancy between the City Accounting and CGSO records no longer exists.

City Accountant to: b.1 reconcile the ledger balance of the CIP accounts with the Quarterly Report on Government PPAs submitted by the CEIDO and record the necessary adjustments, if any; and b.2 draw a Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to recognize to its appropriate asset account/s the completed project totaling P1,497,734.18 and record the corresponding provision for depreciation; and adjust / correct the account Construction in Progress – Infrastructure Assets.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct: a. the City Accountant, in coordination with the Acting City Treasurer, to reconcile the outstanding balance of Loans Payable – Domestic in the financial statements with the balance in the Annual Statement of Indebtedness, Payments and Balances prepared by the City Treasury Management Office, and prepare all necessary adjusting/correcting entries; and b. the City Accountant to classify the outstanding balance of the long-term loans payable in the Statement of Financial Position by re-computing the current and non-current portions of the loan payable based on the Amortization Schedule provided by the Bank and prepare the necessary adjusting/correcting entries.

Also, Accounting Office made correction on 1/31/2021 with JEV No. J000028, debited Prior period Adjustment and credited Loans Payable amounting to P13,074,902.44 due to erroneous Obligation and accounting entry under JEV No. K015992 & Ob. Req. No. 35995 dated 11/28/2019.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to: a. exert effort to retrieve the documents to support the dormant accounts; b. prepare the necessary correcting/adjusting entries on all misclassified account balances; and c. coordinate with the City Treasurer and City Engineer for possible confirmation from various contractors with outstanding retention money and/or the possible reversion to unappropriated surplus of the 10 percent retention money which remained outstanding for three to six years.
observations.managementAction: The City Government Officials concerned are amenable for the recommendations of the Audit Team.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor, as the head of the agency, to strengthen the internal control by installing and implementing additional safeguards over the City’s collections and deposits pursuant to Section 124 of PD No. 1445. observations.managementAction: The City Mayor instructed the concerned Office to take necessary actions to strengthen the internal control in relation to collection and deposits. The Acting City Treasurer ensures close monitoring and supervision in the daily collections and deposits. The City Accountant ensures that Journal Vouchers for transactions without complete supporting documents will not be made.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the: a. City Cooperative and Livelihood Development Officer (CCLDO) to assess the collectability of the long outstanding loans receivables and exert extra effort to collect the outstanding loans receivable; b. City Accountant to set-up the corresponding Allowance for Impairment in the books of accounts based on the assessment by CCLDO; c. City Accountant, if the receivables have been proven to be uncollectible, to derecognize the asset through write-off in accordance with COA Circular No. 2016-005; and d. City Accountant and CCLDO to reconcile the discrepancy between their records and draw JEV for adjustments, if warranted.
observations.managementAction: The City Government Officials concerned are amenable to the recommendations of the Audit Team.
observations.reason: The concerned office is in the process of assessing collectability; full implementation deferred due to COVID-19.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Accountant and the Acting City Treasurer to coordinate with the source agencies and request for the utilization of the remaining balances for other related projects. Otherwise, remit the unutilized balance of P376,700.16 to the concerned source agencies or to the National Treasury.
observations.managementAction: The total unutilized balance of P376,700.16 was remitted to the source agencies and Journal Entry Vouchers Nos. K000932, K000933, K000934 and K011564 were recorded in December 2020 to adjust the balance in the books.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the Acting City Treasurer to increase the City’s fidelity bonds for the City’s accountable officers commensurate to their accountabilities in accordance with the provisions under Treasury Circular No. 02-2009, in order to provide adequate protection on the City Government’s resources.
observations.managementAction: The Acting City Treasurer has increased the fidelity bonds of the City’s accountable officers identified in CY 2020.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated the previous recommendations that the City Mayor direct the Market Supervisor to: a. be more aggressive and persistent in collecting the monthly stall rentals and strictly enforce relevant provisions of the Consolidated Revenue Code; b. maintain subsidiary ledger cards for every individual stallholder and assess penalties for non-payment; c. immediately send out demand letters to delinquent stallholders and after lapse of grace period declare stall vacant and submit to Market Committee for adjudication; and d. post notice of stall vacancies in conspicuous places.
observations.managementAction: The City Government Officials concerned are amenable to the recommendations of the Audit Team.
observations.reason: Market operation in 2020 was temporarily shut down and reduced capacity due to COVID-19, affecting full implementation of recommendations.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Planning and Development Coordinator (CPDC) to: a. coordinate with barangay officials in identifying specific programs, projects and activities (PPAs) during AIP preparation; a.2 ensure only implementable PPAs are included in the AIP; a.3 initiate reprogramming of accumulated unobligated balances of implemented PPAs totaling P1,409,512.48; b. CPDC and City Engineer to determine causes for delays and initiate immediate actions to fully implement PPAs.
observations.managementAction: The CPDO stated that monitoring and evaluation system for infrastructure projects will be strengthen in order to determine the causes for the delay in the implementation. In fact, a Project Monitoring Committee (PMC) was already organized thru Executive Order No. 2019b-32 however, training of the PMC members was delayed due to pandemic and internet connectivity of the members. Coordination with the City Engineer, Bids and Awards Committee and barangay officials concerned will be undertaken to identify the complete descriptions of the specific PPAs and fast track the implementation of the projects. Further, the City Development Council shall review pending projects for implementation to check if still responsive to existing needs of Calambeños.
observations.reason: Training and full implementation of the Project Monitoring Committee delayed due to pandemic and connectivity issues.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Council in coordination with the City Development Council to see to it that the proposed amount per programs, projects and activities (PPAs) included in the Annual Investment Plan (AIP) should be based on the annual budget for the year to ensure full implementation of the PPAs that can protect the community from disasters.
observations.managementAction: The LDRRMF for CY 2020 was P170,000,000.00 or 5% of the total Budget in CY 2020 amounting to P3,400,000,000.00.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Disaster Risk Reduction Management Council to identify the needs of the City in time of calamities and disasters as guide in the preparation of an accurate LDRRMFIP and ensure the maximum utilization of the LDRRMF; and require the CDRRM Officer to closely monitor the implementation of the proposed PPAs and institutionalize measures to strengthen disaster preparedness and response capabilities at all levels.
observations.managementAction: The City Government Officials concerned are amenable to the recommendations of the Audit Team.

observations.reason: The Municipal Engineer contends that the municipality rental rates is higher than the 2014 ACEL rates if the fuel consumption which is shouldered by the lessee is considered in the computation. However, only the computation for the Road Grader was submitted. observations.recommendation: We have recommended that management observe the principle of the DILG in conducting future similar activities within the geographical island where the LGU is located in adherence to the principle of prudent use of scarce financial resources of the government.

Pursuant to COMELEC Minute Resolution No. 19-1054, submit justification that the honoraria granted to the Municipal Board of Canvassers during the May 13, 2019 National and Local Elections is not similar to the honorarium or allowance given by the COMELEC, otherwise the payment shall be disallowed in audit.
observations.managementAction: The management is still negotiating with the COMELEC officer assigned in Calamba during the 2019 Election.
observations.reason:
COMELEC Minute Resolution No. 07-0626 dated March 27, 2007 is superseded by COMELEC Minute Resolution No. 19-1054 dated October 30, 2019, which resolved that the Sangguniang Bayan, Sangguniang Panlungsod,  Sangguniang Panlalawigan of municipalities, cities and provinces, respectively:
May continue to grant an honorarium or allowance to the members of the municipal/city/provincial board of canvassers for the May 13, 2019 National and Local Elections and all subsequent electoral exercises; such honorarium or allowance must not be similar to the honorarium or allowance being given by this Commission; and
May provide in their respective ordinances that of the recipient is already receiving a similar honorarium or allowance for the same service or period, respectively, for which payment is being claimed, the recipient may choose to  receive whichever is higher, but in no case must the recipient receive both amounts.
Hence, the audit team revised the audit recommendation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the CGSO to conduct physical count of inventories semestrally, and to prepare and submit the Report of the Physical Count of Inventories to the Auditor not later than July 31 and January 31 of each year; and the City Accountant to maintain Subsidiary Ledger Cards for each commodity/stock for which is to be reconciled with the GL controlling accounts; and the CGSO to properly maintain and observe correct recording in the SC for each type of inventory; record all receipts and issuances of supplies in the stock cards; consolidate all Requisition and Issue Slip (RIS) for which supplies and materials were issued using the Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued (SSMI) and submit the same to the City Accounting Office; and reconcile the Stock Cards periodically with the ledger cards of Office of the City Accountant; and the City Accountant and the Supply Officer to reconcile the Report of the Physical Count of Inventories with the GL controlling accounts and draw a Journal Entry Voucher to record the adjustments.
observations.managementAction: The City Government Officials concerned are amenable for the recommendations of the Audit Team. The City Accounting Office had already recognized and recorded the said staled checks of General Fund, Special Education Fund and Trust Fund totalling P2,755,875.47 under Journal Entry Vouchers dated January 31, 2020 and June 11, 2020. However, adjustment on the 2019 staled checks was inadvertently recorded as debit to Cash-in-Bank and a credit to Prior Period Adjustment (PPA) instead of debiting PPA and a credit to Accounts Payable since the adjustment was taken in CY 2020. The City Accountant assured that necessary correcting entry will be reflected in July 2020. Also, the City Treasurer will consistently inform and communicate to the payees for their claim except for those unreachable payees already who had either change residence or cannot be contacted on their declared phone numbers. Necessary procedures in reporting staled checks that requires replacement shall likewise be implemented.

Management agreed to conduct the physical inventory taking as soon as all the records are updated. 

A personnel will be requested to be assigned on the required task.
observations.reason:
Not Implemented and Reiterated in 2019AAR.

No report on physical count was submitted.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant and the City
Treasurer to reconcile the balances of their records pertaining to Cash in Bank-
Local Currency account and immediately effect the necessary adjustments to
reflect the correct account balances.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to draw the
adjusting journal entry at year-end to record all staled checks, to wit:
Particulars Debit Credit
Cash in Bank – Local Currency,
Current Account Pxxx
Accounts Payable Pxxx

We recommended that the City Mayor:
a. require the Acting City Treasurer to furnish the City Accountant of a duly
certified list showing the name of taxpayers and the amount due and
collectible for the year as basis in setting up the Real Property Tax
Receivable/Special Education Tax Receivable and corresponding Deferred
Real Property Tax Income/Deferred Special Education Tax Income at the
beginning of the year; and
b. direct the City Accountant to reconcile the RPT/SET Receivables accounts
with the City Treasurer’s list of delinquencies and make necessary
adjustments, if any, to present fairly said accounts in the financial
statements;

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct:
a. the City Accountant to maintain Subsidiary Ledger Cards for each
commodity/stock for which is to be reconciled with the GL controlling
accounts;
b. the CGSO to:
b.1 properly maintain and observe correct recording in the SC for each type of
inventory;
b.2 record all receipts and issuances of supplies in the stock cards;
b.3 consolidate all Requisition and Issue Slip (RIS) for which supplies and
materials were issued using the Summary of Supplies and Materials
Issued (SSMI) and submit the same to the City Accounting Office; and
b.4 reconcile the Stock Cards periodically with the ledger cards of Office of the
City Accountant;
c. the City Accountant and the Supply Officer to:
c.1 to reconcile the Report of the Physical Count of Inventories with the GL
controlling accounts; and
c.2 draw a Journal Entry Voucher to record the adjustments.

We reiterated our recommendations that the City Mayor instruct the:
a. Inventory Committee to continue the conduct of the physical count of all PPE
to determine the existence and completeness of the PPE, prepare the RPCPPE
and submit the same to the Audit Team for further verification;
b. City Accountant and the CGSO to:
b.1 reconcile the amount of PPE per Accounting records with RPCPPE to
ascertain the accuracy of the PPE account balances appearing in
the financial statements;
b.2 verify and evaluate the causes of discrepancies, if any; and
b.3 make the necessary adjustments/corrections in the book of accounts and
property records for the discrepancies noted;
c. CDRRMO Property Officer in coordination with the CGSO to conduct complete
inventory taking of all items purchased and donated for DRRM and prepare the
Inventory List with complete details as to description, quantity and amounts
and submit the same to the Audit Team and the Accounting Unit for monitoring
and record purposes;

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the:
a. City General Services Officer to:
a.1 maintain a complete Local Road Inventory and Road Map;
a.2 keep a Local Road Network Property Card (LRNPC) for all roads and
their components; and
a.3 render a complete Report on Local Road Networks at the end of the
year.
b. City Accountant to:
b.1 reconcile the list of inventory per local road inventory report and road
map with those recorded in the Registry in Public Infrastructure;
b.2 keep and maintain subsidiary records for road and road components
for every road network;
b.3 segregate and recognize the cost of each component of the local
roads with identified cost;
b.4 prepare a Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to record the beginning
balance of the local road network and its components in the general
ledger and the Local Road Network Ledger Card, respectively;
b.5 support the JEV with the Registry of Public Infrastructures for Roads,
working paper on the distribution of costs for the road components;
b.6 prepare a lapsing schedule for the computation of the
depreciation for each component at the end of the year; and
b.7 disclose the total road network system in the Notes to Financial
Statements in the format prescribed under COA Circular No. 2015-008.
c. City Engineer to provide the City Accountant and the City General Services
Officer with the complete description and cost segregation of road
components for road projects.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the:
a. City Engineer to verify the accuracy, existence and current status of the
projects cited and determine the possibility to continue the suspended
projects of which the City has an outstanding payable balance of more than
two years; and
b. City Budget Officer and City Accountant to determine the validity of the
unclaimed amount on projects included in the Accounts Payable and if found
not valid, revert to unappropriated surplus of the City the costs of projects
determined to be non-existent and/or non-implementable.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the:
a. City Accountant and City Budget Officer to revert back to unappropriated
surplus of the City the total amount of P13,240,537.44 determined to be
collected by contractors already;
b. City Accountant, City Treasurer and City Engineer to reconcile their records to
assure the possible reversion to unappropriated surplus in the total amount of
P4,398,787.01 which remained outstanding for three to six years; and
c. City Accountant to:
c.1 effect the necessary adjustments/corrections on the erroneous recording of
the accounts/transactions totalling P1,109,588.77; and
c.2 ascertain the nature of the undetermined difference of P216,547.62
otherwise, revert to unappropriated surplus the said amount.

We recommended that the City Mayor, as the head of the agency, to strengthen
the internal control by installing and implementing additional safeguards over the
City’s collections and deposits pursuant to Section 124 of P.D. No. 1445.

Further, we recommended that the City Mayor direct the Acting City Treasurer to:
a. require collecting officers to remit their collections daily, particularly when
collections exceed P5,000.00 and strictly adhere with the provision under
Section 29 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I;
b. require collecting officers to regularly and diligently prepare the Abstract of
Daily Collections and the subsequent Report on Collections and Deposits;
c. implement a policy on the turn-over of daily collections by the collector to the
liquidating officer. A cut-off may be established on the time of turn-over that
will give the liquidating officer ample time to perform the procedures for the
receipt and verification of collections tuned-over to her and to deposit the
same;
d. likewise, establish a cut-off time in depositing daily collections to the bank by
the liquidating officer to avoid having large balances of daily collections in the
custody of the liquidating officer; and
e. closely monitor and/or supervise the daily collections and deposits.

Moreover, we recommended that the City Mayor require the City Accountant to
refrain from approving JEVs without complete documents to support the
correcting entries on collections and deposits.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the:
a. City Cooperative and Livelihood Development Officer (CCLDO) to assess the
collectability of the long outstanding receivables and exert extra effort to
collect the outstanding loans receivable;
b. City Accountant to set-up the corresponding Allowance for Impairment in the
books of accounts based on the assessed collectability of the long
outstanding receivables;
c. City Accountant, if the receivables have been proven to be uncollectible and
worthless, to derecognize the asset (loan receivable account) through write-
off guided with the provisions under COA Circular No. 2016-005; and
d. City Accountant and the City CCLDO to analyze and reconcile the
discrepancy noted between their records and to draw a Journal Entry
Voucher for the adjustments, if warranted.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the:
a. City Accountant to reconcile her records with the City Treasurer’s Office and
City Assessor’s Office and effect the necessary adjustments/corrections in the
books, if warranted; and
b. Acting City Treasurer to:
b.1 review the list of tax delinquencies and coordinate with the City Assessor
to determine the validity, existence and ownership of the properties; and
b.2 enforce remedies provided under RA No. 7160 such as administrative
action thru warrant of levy or judicial action to further increase its tax
collections and consequently generate additional income that would fund
more developmental programs and projects for the general welfare of the
public.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Accountant and the Acting
City Treasurer to coordinate with the source agencies and request for the
utilization of the remaining balances for other related projects. Otherwise, remit
the unutilized balance of P376,700.16 to the concerned source agencies or to the
National Treasury.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Treasurer and City
Accountant to grant cash advance to authorized Disbursing Officer only.

We recommended that the City Mayor and the City Superintendent of Schools, as
co-chairmen of the Local School Board to:
a. submit to the Office of the Auditor documents and justifications as to why
expenses totalling P32,475,272.58 were charged against the City’s Special
Education Fund (SEF); and
b. allow only procurement authorized under Section 4.0 of DepEd-DBM-DILG JMC
No. 1, series of 2017.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the Acting City Treasurer to increase
the fidelity bonds of the City’s accountable officers commensurate to their
accountabilities in accordance with the provisions under Treasury Circular No. 02-
2009, in order to provide adequate protection on the City Government’s resources.

We reiterated our previous year’s recommendations that the City Mayor direct the
Market Supervisor to:
a. be more aggressive and persistent in collecting the monthly stall rentals and
strictly enforce Sections 5B.06 and 5B.08 of the 2006 Revised and
Consolidated Revenue Code of the City of Calamba;
b. maintain subsidiary ledger cards for every individual stall holders, examine if
there are delinquent stallholders and assess their penalties for the non-
payment of rent as provided in Section 5B.06 of the same Consolidated
Revenue Code;
c. immediately send out demand letters to delinquent stallholders to pay their
arrears, and after the lapse of grace period, declare the stall vacant and
submit the same to the Market Committee headed by the City Mayor as
Chairman for proper adjudication, notwithstanding the immediate filing of
appropriate legal charges to those stall holders who failed to pay their arrears
after due demand; and
d. post notice of stall vacancies in conspicuous places such as unoccupied stall
or booth and in the bulletin board of the market.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Market Administrator, in
coordination with the City Legal Officer to:
a. conduct an investigation of the reported stalls with sub-lessees as provided in
Appendix O;
b. if found that the lessee indicated in the contracts are not in reality occupying
but instead sub-leases their stalls, cancel/terminate the Contract of Lease of
the registered stallholders pursuant Section 5B.08 (e) of the 2006 Revised and
Consolidated Revenue Code of the City Government; and
c. if the actual occupants of the stalls are qualified under the City’s existing
rules and regulations immediately send out the “Application to Lease Market
Stalls” as provided in Section 5B.08 of the same Consolidated Revenue Code.
Subsequently enter into contract of lease stipulating the duration of lease,
rental rate, and use of leased premises and transfer of rights or lease
assignment, among others.

We reiterated our recommendation that the City Mayor instruct the:
a. City Planning and Development Coordinator (CPDC) to:
a.1 coordinate with barangay officials in identifying the specific programs,
projects and activities (PPAs) during the preparation of the City’s Annual
Investment Program (AIP) funded out of the 20% Development Fund in
order to determine the possibility of implementing the PPAs;
a.2 ensure that only implementable PPAs are included in the AIP; and
a.3 initiate the reprogramming of the accumulated unobligated balances of
the implemented PPAs totalling P1,409,512.48 for the optimum utilization
of the fund.
b. CPDC and the City Engineer to determine the cause/s for the delay and/or
non-implementation of the 48 other infrastructure projects and initiate
immediate action/s to fully implement the PPAs.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Development Council in
coordination with the City Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Council
(CDRRMC) to see to it that the proposed amount per programs, projects and
activities (PPAs) included in the Annual Investment Plan (AIP) should be based on
the total annual budget for the year to ensure full implementation of the PPAs that
can protect the community from disasters.

We have recommended that management:
Require the Municipal Assessor to furnish the Municipal Treasurer the revised assessment roll so that the latter could prepare the updated RPTAR/Certified List of Taxpayers; and
Instruct the Municipal Accountant to draw a Journal Entry Voucher to adjust the affected accounts.

We reiterated our prior year’s audit recommendations that management:
Conduct actual physical inventory-taking of the properties and prepare Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE);
Require the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to maintain Property Cards, Property, Plant and Equipment Ledger Cards for each category of assets, and Real Property Ledger Cards for land; and
Require them further to conduct periodic reconciliation of the ledger card balances in order to achieve accuracy and fair presentation of the financial statements.

We reiterated our prior year’s audit recommendation that management instruct the Municipal Assessor to secure and commence the processing of the TCT of all the land properties, and seek budgetary allocations for the pertinent cost thereof; and
Direct the Municipal Assessor to provide copies of TCT to the Municipal Accountant, as basis for the latter to establish a subsidiary list and as basis to elect the revaluation model in accounting for Land as prescribed under IPSAS 17 using latest available Fair Market Value.
We have recommended that management submit to COA copies of the Memorandum of Agreement on aforementioned fund transfers and effect the submission thereon whenever future fund transfers are involved.

We have recommended that management observe the principle of the DILG in conducting future similar activities within the geographical island where the LGU is located in adherence to the principle of prudent use of scarce financial resources of the government.

We have recommended that management provide legal basis citing that the said payment of honoraria to the MBOC shall be sourced from the LGU fund and submit a certificate of non-payment of honoraria from the COMELEC Central Office Accountant.  Otherwise, bill the COMELEC for the total election expenses incurred during the May 13, 2019 National and Local Elections in accordance with Section 10 of the Omnibus Election Code.

We have recommended that management strictly require the contractors to post the prescribed warranty security for structural defectsand failures which will remain effective for one (1) year from the date of issuance of the Certificate of Final Acceptance by theLGU and returned only after the lapse of the said one (1) year periodin accordance with Sections 62.2.3.3 and 62.2.3.4 of the Revised IRR of R.A. 9184.

observations.managementAction: The Inventory Committee continue the conduct of the physical count of all PPE to determine the existence and completeness of the PPE. The City Accountant and the CGSO reconciled their records. Necessary adjustments in the books of accounts were already recorded. Reconciliation of stock cards and subsidiary ledgers are properly up-dated.
observations.reason: Based on the Summary Report of the Actual Physical Count of PPE for General Fund and Special Education Fund submitted by the GSO as of December 31, 2019, the total variance between the books of account of Department and GSO records showed a decrease of 79% or P806,771,612.53 as compared to CY 2018 variance of P3,842,723,021.78.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor: a) instruct the Acting City Treasurer and City Accountant to reconcile their records with the HDMF and GSIS to determine the correct amount to be deducted to the employees and remit all premiums/contributions and loan amortizations due to HDMF or Pag-IBIG Fund and GSIS in accordance with their base system; and b) direct the City Accountant to maintain Subsidiary Ledgers for employees with outstanding obligations to HMDF and GSIS to ascertain the accuracy of the balances of Due to Pag-IBIG and Due to GSIS accounts appearing in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: The City Government Officials concerned are amenable for the recommendations of the Audit Team. The unremitted amount due to Pag-IBIG or Pag-IBIG Fund were already remitted last January 10, 2019 under check No. 229969 dated December 28, 2018. The unremitted amount of P74,186.44 was remitted on June 14, 2019 under Official Receipts Nos. 005000032532, 0005000032535, 00050000325233, and 0005000032534. The amount of P163,949.26 were already refunded to various employees under Payroll Register-January to December 2018 GSIS Refund. The City Accounting Office see to it that individual subsidiary ledgers of employees for HDMF and HMDF are properly maintained.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to: a) reclassify the non-current portion of long-term loans for settlement within one year as current and present the non-current portion of loans due for settlement within more than one year as non-current liabilities as required under the MNGAS for LGUs; and b) disclose the required financial information in the Notes to Financial Statements to assist users in understanding the financial statements and to provide them with additional information value.
observations.managementAction: The City Accountant had already made a correct reclassification of non-current portion of long-term loans payables to non-current liabilities as required under the MNGAS for LGUs. The significant information in the Notes to Financial Statements were already disclosed.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor: a) instruct the City Planning and Budget Officer to maintain individual RAAOPS, RAAOMO and RAAOCO for each economic enterprise; and b) require the City Accountant to maintain subsidiary ledgers for each economic enterprise; prepare separate financial statements and schedules for each economic enterprise to form part of the schedules of the General Fund trial balance to serve as a tool in informing management whether or not the income generated by the economic enterprises are sufficient to meet their operating costs and to serve as basis in improving the operation of the economic enterprise.
observations.managementAction: The City Budget Office already maintained individual RAAOPS, RAAOMO and RAAOCO for each economic enterprise. The City Accounting Office is maintaining subsidiary ledgers. Separate financial statements and schedules for each economic enterprise were already prepared.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor: a) require the Acting City Treasurer to transfer immediately at the end of the year the fund equivalent to the unexpended/unutilized balance of the LDRRMF from the General Fund to the Trust Fund book; and b) instruct the City Accountant to disclose in the Notes to Financial Statements the amount and details of the unexpended balance of the LDRRMF.
observations.managementAction: The City Treasurer had already transferred the amount of P18,895,573.92 from General Fund Account to Trust Fund Account as per letter of request addressed to the Land Bank of the Philippines dated March 20, 2019. The City Accountant already disclosed the amount and details of the unexpended balance of the LDRRMF in the year-end Notes to Financial Statements.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor: a) require the Acting City Treasurer to direct the Liquidating Officer to see to it that the amount of cash and checks deposited shall reconcile with the total cash and checks remitted by the Collecting Officers; and deposit all collections daily and intact to prevent accumulation of cash in her possession which may result to possible misuse and lapping; b) direct the Acting City Treasurer and City Accountant to submit to the Audit Team copies of deposit slips with list of checks for all check deposits relative to the JEVs issued by the City Accounting Office as correcting entries to cover up the undeposited collections of P20,883,789.72; and c) require the City Accountant to refrain from approving JEVs without complete documents to support the correcting entries on collections and deposits.
observations.managementAction: The City Treasury Management Office admittedly encountered some lapses on the internal control imposed over the collections and deposits of the City Government and in relation thereto, the City Treasurer assure stricter internal control to ascertain the reliability of the LGUs process. Collectors/tellers shall accomplish their daily turnover of all their collections intact and on time to the Local Treasurer/Cashier. Collectors/tellers are mandated to accomplish the Daily Report of Collections, validate the correctness of remittances and adhere to the cut off time for turn-over of collections to the Liquidating Officer with stricter control and attention.
observations.reason: The collections from December 27 to 30, 2019 amounting to P72,763,841.05 were deposited on January 2 to 6, 2020 and thus were not deposited daily as required.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the Human Resource Management Officer (HRMO) to require all JO workers to submit Accomplishment Reports and DTRs and to see to it that these documents are attached to the payrolls to ascertain the propriety of the disbursements for JOs.
observations.managementAction: The City Human Resource Management Officer on this matter. The HRMO complied on this matter.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) Secretariat, City Technical Specialist and the General Services Officer (GSO) to immediately submit the aforesaid documents and comply strictly with the guidelines in the submission of contracts and Purchase Orders to COA so that any deficiency/ies that can be found in the evaluation, review and inspection of contracts can be immediately rectified.
observations.managementAction: The City Engineering Office had already submitted to the COA Technical Audit Specialist the necessary documents pertinent to the mentioned infrastructures projects of the City Government on April 26, 2019.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor: a) instruct the City Planning and Development Coordinator (CPDC) and the City Engineer to determine and address the cause/s for the delay and/or non-implementation of the 166 projects with total amount of P466,747,818.95; and b) employ regular monitoring mechanisms in assessing the rate of progress of project implementation and undertake a closer coordination with the City Planning and Development Officials to ensure that programmed projects are implemented and completed on time.
observations.managementAction: A strategic brainstorming was conducted in order to achieve the required objectives. The City Government assured that close monitoring of on-going projects will be prioritized. The City is still in the process of determining how to effectively implement the PPAs. A strategic brainstorming was conducted to achieve the required objectives.
observations.reason: Some of the unexpended balances out of completed projects were re-programmed to various priority projects of the City Government in CY 2020.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the CSWMB review and update the Ten-Year CSWMP every two years thereafter or as the need arises to ensure the ten years sustainability, viability and effectiveness of the solid waste management strategy of the City to meet the objectives of the Plan.
observations.managementAction: The CENRO together with the CSWMB conducted periodic meetings in order to assess and monitor their timely accomplishments.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the Market Supervisor to: a) be more aggressive and persistent in collecting the monthly stall rentals and strictly enforce Sections 5B.06 and 5B.08 of the 2006 Revised and Consolidated Revenue Code of the City of Calamba; b) maintain subsidiary ledger cards for every individual stall holders, examine if there are delinquent stallholders and assess their penalties for the non-payment of rent as provided in Section 5B.06 of the same Consolidated Revenue Code; c) immediately send out demand letters to delinquent stallholders to pay their arrears, and after the lapse of grace period, declare the stall vacant and submit the same to the Market Committee headed by the City Mayor as Chairman for proper adjudication, notwithstanding the immediate filing of appropriate legal charges to those stall holders who failed to pay their arrears after due demand; d) post notice of stall vacancies in conspicuous places such as unoccupied stall or booth and in the bulletin board of the market; e) stop allowing dummies sub-lease of stalls and enforce strict implementation of Section 5B.08 of the same Consolidated Revenue Code; f) maintain subsidiary ledger cards for every individual stallholder; and g) submit a written explanation on the discrepancy of balances of P1,228,482.00 between the estimated collections based on the submitted list and the annual market fee as per report submitted by the Market Supervisor.
observations.managementAction: The City Government Officials concerned are amenable for the recommendations of the Audit Team. The Market Administrator made postings of notices of vacancies to all unoccupied stalls. Collectors were instructed to strictly monitor the monthly rentals. The Market Management was updating the filing of subsidiary ledger cards.
observations.reason: Filing of subsidiary ledger cards were not yet completed. Reconcilation between Treasury records and Accounting book balances were being reconciled and reconciliation is not yet completed.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Engineer to: a) take appropriate actions so that the Solar Panel System can be generating additional source of deduction to the Meralco electrical billings; and b) ensure that the electrical panel boards as well as the inverters and other electrical system of the solar panel system are reinstalled afar from the place where the existing electrical machine/pump room is currently installed to eliminate the risk of being damaged or destroyed when flooding occurs.
observations.managementAction: The City Engineering Office had constructed a wall that separates the machine/pump room from the electrical panel boards and other electrical system of the solar panel. They also assured that the electrical panel boards are safe in case there is a leak from machine/pump room. Significant decrease in Meralco billing was recorded. However, increase in the current electrical billing is due to installation of new air-conditioning units in different offices.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor ensure that the annual GAD programs and activities are fully utilizing all available appropriations.
observations.managementAction: The unimplemented GAD projects for CY 2018 were on-going and estimated to be completed within five years of implementation; for those requiring long-time benefits, the GAD projects for CY 2019 were completed as of December 31, 2019. Hence the balances for GAD Projects for CY 2019 require a long-time for completion.

observations.reason:
Partially Implemented but Reiterated in 2019 AAR.  A TCT has been secured for one land property while the titles of other properties are still being processed.

Some MOAs were submitted after the exit conference.

Enjoin the Municipal Development Council to approve project proposals that are detailed for inclusion in its Municipal Development Fund

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our prior year’s audit recommendation that management:
Conduct actual physical inventory-taking of the properties and prepare Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE) be prepared and submitted;
Require the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to maintain Property Cards, Property, Plant and Equipment Ledger Cards for each category of assets, and Real Property Ledger Cards for land; and
Require them further to conduct periodic reconciliation of the ledger card balances in order to achieve accuracy and fair presentation of the financial statements.

We recommended that management submit to COA copies of the Memorandum of Agreement on aforementioned fund transfers and effect the submission thereon whenever future fund transfers are involved.
Management Comment:
Management promised to submit MOA on fund transfers within fifteen days after the exit conference and agreed to submit henceforth within 5 working days from execution all succeeding contracts/ MOAs.

We recommended that the management:
Re-program the unused funds; and
Enjoin the Municipal Development Council to approve project proposals that are detailed for inclusion in its Municipal Development Fund
Management Comment:
Management submitted an SB Resolution re-programming said unused funds.

We reiterated our prior year’s audit recommendation that   management study and enjoin the Sangguniang Bayan to strengthen its existing policies and ordinances to govern its public utilities and economic enterprises. Likewise, we recommended that management instruct the concerned municipal officials (i.e. accountant, treasurer, budget officer, CWS Manager) for the creation of special accounts for economic enterprises and observe relevant reporting requirements thereon.
Management Comment:
The Water System Manager promised to submit a financial report for the operations this CY 2019.

We recommended that the management:
Direct the CWS Manager to submit a written request or appeal from appropriate DPWH office for the refund of the amount paid or the replacement of items procured; and
Instruct concerned officials to enter into ADRRI with the IO on upcoming projects and demand immediate compliance from the latter should the local government be confronted in like detrimental circumstances.
Management Comment:
Management concurred with the audit recommendation.

We recommended that management:
Make representation to seek assistance from GAD Resource Persons, such as those accredited by the Phil. Commission on Women, for the conduct or participation of a training and workshop on GAD Planning, with emphasis on the use of GAD tools; and
Improve and develop the existing GAD Database or Sex-Disaggregated Data for proper utilization in the planning, budgeting, programming, and policy formulation of the Municipality, as well as proper charging of the object of expenditures on actual accomplishments.
Management Comment:
Management concurred with the audit recommendation.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended to the Agency Head the following:

1. Direct the Municipal Treasurer to post the Notice of Delinquency in the Payment of Real Property Tax and other taxes in a publicly accessible and conspicuous place in each barangay of the municipal government;

2. Also, to avail of the civil remedies provided for in Chapter IV, Title I, Book II of R.A. 7160 and develop other feasible strategies to enhance collections of delinquent taxes from taxpayers; and

To conduct periodic reconciliation of Real Property Tax Receivable with the Accountant and Assessor and to establish database for each individual Real Property Taxpayers.
observations.managementAction: The municipal Assessor has regularly furnished the Municipal Treasurer copy of the quarterly report of Real Property Assessment (QRRPA) and copy of the assessment roll for their reference on current real property tax collectibles
observations.reason:
Partially Implemented

Posting of delinquencies were made but records are yet to be submitted as to how much of the delinquencies were collected.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to expedite the analysis and recording of long outstanding reconciling items to their appropriate accounts; take up the reconciling items in the books immediately after the preparation of the monthly Bank Reconciliation Statements to avoid accumulated adjustments; and immediately communicate bank errors with bank officials as soon as such errors are discovered.
We also recommended that the City Treasurer notify the payees/check holders one month before the check will become stale.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Cooperatives and Livelihood Development Department Head and the City Accountant to:
reconcile their records, analyze the accounts receivable and update the status of collection;
send demand letters to borrowers regularly to remind them of their obligations; and
continue to vigorously pursue efforts to collect the receivables to maintain the sustainability of the program.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to:
analyze the Accounts Payable and revert to the Unappropriated Surplus of the General Fund those that proved to be with no actual claims, administrative or judicial, or which is not covered with perfected contracts on record; and
conduct periodic review and monitoring of the Accounts Payable so that this account will consist only of valid claims against the City Government.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Officer to prepare and submit to the Office of the Auditor the Report on Utilization of Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund on or before the 15th day after the end of each month using the format prescribed under Annex B of COA Circular No. 2012-002.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to disclose the required financial information in the Notes to Financial Statement to assist the users in understanding the financial statements and to provide them with additional information value.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Treasurer and the OIC-City Accountant to remit immediately the amount of P903,062.56 due the barangays, after which, ensure that barangays’ shares from RPT and CTC are released within five days after the end of each quarter as mandated under Section 271(d) of the Local Government Code and in line with Section 15 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I.

We recommended the City Mayor require the accountable officers to submit the paid disbursement vouchers, financial records and other related reports to the Audit Team within ten days of the ensuing month as required under Section 7.2.1 of the RRSA prescribed under Circular No. 2009-006 dated September 15, 2009. As regards the submission of financial reports such as Trial Balance and related reports, direct the Accountant to strictly comply with the aforementioned provisions of the MNGAS, Volume I for LGUs.
We also recommended that actions be taken against those responsible for the delayed submission of accounts.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the Chief Accountant and the General Services Officer to strictly adopt the Perpetual Inventory Method in accounting for inventory items.
Likewise, we recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to draw a Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) for the proposed adjustment of the inventory accounts of the City government.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the CSSD to:
draw appropriate plans by identifying and consolidating the items needed in the implementation of the Supplementary Feeding Program and consider procurement through public bidding, except  as highly exceptional cases and justified by the conditions set forth in Rule XVI of RA No. 9184. Should public bidding be adopted, the BAC shall post an Invitation to Bid or Request for Quotations in the PhilGEPS website of the City Government and in conspicuous places within the premises of the City, to encourage participation of interested bidder in the public bidding or submission their price quotation;
stop the practice of procuring goods through the grant of cash advance, pursuant to COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997; and
stop the practice of transferring of cash advance from one person to another.

We recommended that the City Local School Board refrain from using the Special Education Fund for purposes other than those authorized under Section 1 of RA No. 5447, Sections 100 and 272 of RA No. 7160 and Joint Circular No. 01, s. 1998 dated April 14, 1998 in order not to defeat the purpose for which SEF was created and   deplete the fund for authorized SEF programs, projects and activities.

We recommended that payment for regular expenses be made in check directly to the Suppliers/Dealers. Stop the practice of payment in cash by City officials, subject to reimbursement, pursuant to COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997.
We also recommended that appropriate procurement procedure provided under RA No. 9184 be strictly observed for the procurement of goods and services.

We recommended that the City Mayor and other officials submit the liquidation report on cash advances for confidential expenses amounting to ₱42,715,150.68 to the Intelligence, Confidential Fund Audit Unit (ICFAU), Office of the Chairman, as required under Joint Circular No. 2015-01 of COA, DBM, DILG, GCG and DND dated January 8, 2015.
We also recommended that the City Councilor be required to refund cash advance for his travel and training totaling ₱30,446.00 considering that the travel was cancelled. Advise the City Accountant to ensure that all cash advances are fully liquidated at the end of the year.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Accountant to reclassify the fund transfer of P2,550.000.00 from Other Payables account to Due to NGAs account, in compliance with COA Circular No. 2015-009 to ensure fair presentation of both accounts.
We also recommended that the City Mayor require the City Accountant to submit the Report on the Receipt and Utilization of the Fund Transfer for CY 2016 for the verification and determination of the status of fund.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Planning and Development Office (CPDO) to submit appropriate project proposal to fully utilize the BUB fund to realize immediate benefits thereon by the intended beneficiaries. Otherwise, we recommended that the fund be returned to the source agency so it can be put to use for urgent projects of other agencies needing financial assistance

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City General Services Officer and the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) thru its Secretariat to furnish the Auditor with copies of contracts and Purchase Orders with the supporting documents within five days from the issuance/perfection thereof, in compliance with COA Circular No. 2009-001.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to set up RPT and SEF Receivables based on the certified list of taxpayers and the amount due and collectible for a particular year, in compliance with Section 20 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I, to be able to establish with certainty the reported RPT/SET Receivable balances.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Accountant to verify and address the differences that exist between the amounts set up for Real Property Tax Receivables and Special Education Tax Receivables accounts, as well as their respective collections, and make the necessary adjustments; and henceforth, always ensure the equality of amounts set up and collected for Real Property Tax and Special Education Tax Receivables.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Treasurer to enforce collection of the delinquent Real Property Taxes by conducting tax campaign and granting incentives or relief to taxpayers thru compromise agreement, with the authority of the Sangguniang Panlungsod, otherwise, avail the remedies provided under Sections 254 and 256 of RA No. 7160.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Treasurer to require every Disbursing Officer to maintain a cashbook for each fund and ensure that a certification is written in the cashbook at the end of the month or when required to do so by proper competent authority, to comply with Sections 25 and 28 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume II and Section 174, Article 2, Title 4, Book III, GAAM.
We recommended that the City Mayor require the refund of the excess amount of P547,094.03 by the concerned officials.

We recommended that Management allocate 1% of IRA for the strengthening and implementation of the PPAs of the Local Councils for the Protection of Children as required in the DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2012-120.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Planning and Development Office to see to it that the 20% Development Fund is optimally utilized to help achieve desirable socio-economic development and environmental outcomes for the general public, and that only expenditures authorized under Section 2.3 of DILG-DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2011-1 dated April 13, 2011 are charged against the Fund.

We have recommended to management that the Municipal Accountant assign personnel to regularly get the copies of bank statements at the bank and prepare the required monthly Bank Reconciliation Statements (BRS) so that all valid reconciling items be properly taken up in the books to ensure compliance to COA Circular No. 96-011 dated October 2, 1996 and establish reliability of cash account.
We have recommended to the Agency Head to direct the accountable officer to remit collections intact daily in consonance with Section 69 of PD 1445 and to avoid possible misappropriation of funds.

We reiterated our recommendations that the City Mayor direct:

The OIC - City Accountant to conduct a thorough review of records to determine the validity of all reconciling items and come up with the correct balance of Cash in Bank accounts, otherwise, said reconciling items will be considered shortage/overage of the City Treasury.


the OIC – City Accountant to properly notify the depository banks of those items that need adjustments within ten days upon receipt of the Bank Statement to immediately address the cause/s of the discrepancy and accordingly effect adjustment to eliminate errors.

c. exert effort to reconcile the cashbooks with the Accounting records on a regular basis to support the correctness of the Cash in Bank account.
observations.managementAction:
AAR 2015 | The OIC-City Accountant has already drawn the necessary adjustments on the items revealed in the BRS as of December 31, 2015. Such adjustments were recorded in the books of accounts on December 31, 2016 with its supporting Journal Entry Vouchers, thus resulting in reduction in the net reconciling items in the BRS.

The depository banks were notified on the necessary adjustments on the items that need immediate actions. Causes of discrepancies were thoroughly explained to avoid repetitive occurrence of errors.






The cashbook balances for CY 2016 are already tallied with the adjusted book balances of the BRS.
observations.reason: Reiterated in Part II, Observation No. 1 of this report.

We also recommended that expenses be kept within the limit of income earned in pursuit of economical operation.
observations.managementAction:
Management officials have already implemented this recommendation. A new Market Management Committee (NMMC) was created under Executive Order No. 16b-03, series of 2016 to handle the awarding of the said certificates of award, specifically to those stalls that were satisfactorily identified as valid and legal by the previous Market Management Committee.  

All stalls identified as vacant are marked by posting a notice of its availability.

The market office has on file individual records of delinquent payees wherein a schedule could be extracted.

The market office at the Mercado de Calamba has an updated list of applicants on file. 

The enterprise is really keeping its expenses tightly. Vacated positions whether regular or Job Order are not being filled-up or replaced. Operational expenses, particularly utilities, are being kept to its minimum in order to attain economical operation.
